aliases: 糯米糍 / 椰蓉糯米糍

chinese: 糯米糍 / 椰蓉糯米糍

food_subgroup: Chinese / Cantonese Dim Sum and Desserts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for coconut mochi (Lo Mai Chi). Closest match is glutinous rice cake food product. Coconut coating and peanut/red bean filling are not captured by this node.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Lo Mai Chi. A0EKP (rice-based confectionery) is the closest defensible anchor. Coconut coating and sweet filling are not distinguished.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Cantonese confection.

allergens: gluten (glutinous rice flour may contain trace gluten)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Coconut Mochi is one ball weighing approximately 50 g, as commonly served at dim sum restaurants and Cantonese bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mochi, rice cake (glutinous rice confection) and coconut-based snack composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Coconut Mochi (Lo Mai Chi) exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for glutinous rice flour (FDC ID 169717), desiccated coconut (FDC ID 169120), and peanut-sugar filling analogues, weighted to reflect a typical Lo Mai Chi formulation (approx. 55% glutinous rice dough, 25% peanut-sugar filling, 20% desiccated coconut coating). The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor adjustments. Saturated fat estimated from coconut and peanut fat profiles. Cholesterol confirmed zero for this plant-based product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice and glutinous rice-based products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice (GI ~98) and mochi-type confections (GI ~80–90)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Coconut Mochi (Lo Mai Chi) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 85 is estimated from the database entries for glutinous rice (GI ~98, University of Sydney) and mochi-type glutinous rice confections (GI ~80–88, University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The peanut and coconut filling moderately lowers the overall GI relative to plain glutinous rice. GL per serving calculated as (85 × 26.25 g available carbohydrate per 50 g serving) / 100 = 22.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (45 mg per 100 g; ~22.5 mg per 50 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Coconut Mochi is very low in sodium and does not pose a meaningful blood pressure risk; however, individuals managing hypertension should remain mindful of total caloric intake given the moderate energy density of 340 kcal per 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~85) and high sugar content (18.5 g per 100 g) from glutinous rice flour and added sugar in filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coconut Mochi has a high glycemic index of approximately 85 and a glycemic load of ~22 per 50 g serving, making it likely to cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption and monitor blood glucose closely if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content (~5.8 g per 100 g) from desiccated coconut and peanut filling, combined with high sugar and refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from coconut and the high refined carbohydrate and sugar content of Coconut Mochi are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption as part of an overall heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut Mochi, known in Cantonese as 糯米糍 (Lo Mai Chi), is a classic Cantonese dim sum item and traditional dessert originating from Southern China (Guangdong province). It consists of a soft, pillowy, and slightly chewy outer shell made from steamed glutinous rice flour dough, which is wrapped around a sweet filling — most commonly a crunchy mixture of ground peanuts, sugar, and sesame seeds, or alternatively a smooth red bean paste. The finished ball is rolled in desiccated coconut, which prevents sticking, adds a delicate tropical aroma, and provides a subtle textural contrast to the soft interior. The dish is celebrated for its 'QQ' (bouncy, elastic) texture, a hallmark of quality glutinous rice preparations. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 340 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 12.5 g fat (of which ~5.8 g is saturated, largely from coconut), and 52.5 g carbohydrates including 18.5 g sugar. Dietary fibre is modest at 2.2 g. Sodium is very low at 45 mg. The glycemic index is high (~85), making this a high-glycemic food that can cause rapid blood glucose elevation — a significant concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat from coconut is also relevant for cardiovascular health. It is popular at tea houses, dim sum restaurants, and bakeries across Southern China, Hong Kong, and Southeast Asia, and is commonly served as a snack or dessert at any time of day.
common_user_queries: What is coconut mochi made of?
How many calories in coconut mochi?
What is the GI of mochi?
What is the difference between mochi and Lo Mai Chi?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Coconut Mochi (Lo Mai Chi) was found in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component entries for glutinous rice flour, desiccated coconut, and peanut-sugar filling, weighted to a typical Lo Mai Chi formulation. The input AI-generated values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice and mochi-type confections. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture data for the composite Lo Mai Chi preparation is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17; individual component moisture values vary significantly depending on steaming duration and dough hydration ratio, and no consolidated moisture figure for this traditional preparation exists in either approved source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: this is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived ingredients in the standard peanut-filling formulation; both values are confirmed zero by the USDA FoodData Central component data for glutinous rice flour and desiccated coconut. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this traditional steamed preparation with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based product. Saturated fat (5.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.1 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles for desiccated coconut and peanuts weighted to the typical formulation; these are approximate. GI of 85 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice (~98) and mochi-type confections (~80–88); no direct measurement for Lo Mai Chi exists in the database. GL per 50 g serving = (85 × 26.25) / 100 = 22.3. The input source cited (South China University of Technology, http://www.scut.edu.cn/) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence. Nutrient values are grounded in USDA FoodData Central adapted composite. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 50 g entries) noted; deduplicated to single 50 g serving. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315091 is the closest available node (glutinous rice cake food product); no exact node for Lo Mai Chi exists. FoodEx2 code A0EKP used as closest rice-based confectionery anchor.
